title: Optimization Rollout Health Dashboard (Safe Apply + Rollback)
preview:
## Overview
This dashboard is the primary operational view for monitoring **Optimization Config** rollouts (batching, caching, quantization profiles) across Hosted, Dedicated, and (where applicable) Private environments. It is designed to support the **Safe Apply** workflow by:
- Making rollout health obvious at a g...
text:
## Overview
This dashboard is the primary operational view for monitoring **Optimization Config** rollouts (batching, caching, quantization profiles) across Hosted, Dedicated, and (where applicable) Private environments. It is designed to support the **Safe Apply** workflow by:
- Making rollout health obvious at a glance (error/latency/regression/saturation).
- Providing drill-down by **tenant/model/region/config_version**.
- Annotating charts with rollout lifecycle events (validate → canary → promote → rollback) to speed incident triage.

**Primary users:** SRE on-call, Platform Control Plane operators, Runtime on-call.

**Source of truth for rollout state:** Optimization Config Service + rollout controller.

---

## Dashboard location
- **Console name:** Optimization Rollout Health
- **Grafana folder:** Platform → Rollouts
- **Dashboard ID:** `opt-rollout-health`

---

## When to us...

title: Region Health Service (RHS) — Aggregated Region Health for Routing and Failover
preview:
## Overview
The Region Health Service (RHS) provides a single, consistent, low-latency view of per-region health for Redwood’s multi-region routing and failover automation. RHS aggregates multiple signals (reachability, error/latency burn, capacity/headroom, and control-plane dependencies) into a normalized region s...
text:
## Overview
The Region Health Service (RHS) provides a single, consistent, low-latency view of per-region health for Redwood’s multi-region routing and failover automation. RHS aggregates multiple signals (reachability, error/latency burn, capacity/headroom, and control-plane dependencies) into a normalized region status with explainability ("why we think a region is unhealthy").

RHS is designed to:
- Reduce failover trigger noise by requiring corroboration and applying dampening/hold-down logic upstream of policy changes.
- Provide deterministic inputs for the routing policy engine and the failover controller.
- Provide an auditable “evidence bundle” to support on-call investigation and post-incident reviews.

**Primary consumers**
- Smart Routing / Policy Engine (real-time routing decisions)
- Failover Controller (automation decisioning + execution)
- Observability dashboards/alert...

title: Operator mental model cards and scenario decks
preview:
Overview
--------
This page defines the canonical format and maintenance process for 'mental model cards' and 'scenario decks' — concise, operator-facing artifacts intended to accelerate safe decisions during platform incidents and routine investigations. These are one-page cognitive aids (cards) plus short scenario...
text:
Overview
--------
This page defines the canonical format and maintenance process for 'mental model cards' and 'scenario decks' — concise, operator-facing artifacts intended to accelerate safe decisions during platform incidents and routine investigations. These are one-page cognitive aids (cards) plus short scenario packs used for training and post-incident rehearsals.

Why this exists
----------------
- Operators are often forced to make time-sensitive decisions with partial telemetry. Well designed, compact mental models reduce cognitive load and speed correct action.
- Cards codify heuristics that experienced operators use; scenario decks let us rehearse edge cases without heavy prep.

Audience and scope
------------------
- Primary: platform operators, oncall SREs, and platform on-call trainers.
- Secondary: platform product managers, support escalation engineers, and solutions en...

title: Adaptive Tenant Scorecard and Auto-Buydown for Throttling
preview:
Overview
========
This document describes the Adaptive Tenant Scorecard (ATS) — a lightweight, realtime heuristic that ranks tenants by operational risk — and the Auto-Buydown (AB) action that temporarily reduces a tenant's burst/priority during system stress. The goal is to protect platform SLOs while minimizing ma...
text:
Overview
========
This document describes the Adaptive Tenant Scorecard (ATS) — a lightweight, realtime heuristic that ranks tenants by operational risk — and the Auto-Buydown (AB) action that temporarily reduces a tenant's burst/priority during system stress. The goal is to protect platform SLOs while minimizing manual intervention and preserving customer experience for high-value tenants.

Scope
-----
- Applies to multi-tenant inference pools managed by platform scheduler: service name: "redwood-infer-pool-v2".
- Covers transient throttling (soft buydown) actions and automatic recovery.
- Does NOT replace contractual hard throttles (billing/commit enforcement) or Dedicated/Private tenant controls.

Motivation & goals
------------------
1. Avoid system-wide SLO degradation (target: 99.9% request success and p95 latency <= 450ms across public pools).
2. Provide predictable, auditable ...

title: Emergent Tenant Behavior Simulation and Operator Playbook
preview:
Summary:
This runbook describes the Emergent Tenant Behavior Simulation (ETBS) harness, the operator playbook for diagnosing and mitigating cross-tenant emergent failures, and the dashboards/alerts recommended for early detection. ETBS is a lightweight lab and production-adjacent simulator used to reproduce tenant i...
text:
Summary:
This runbook describes the Emergent Tenant Behavior Simulation (ETBS) harness, the operator playbook for diagnosing and mitigating cross-tenant emergent failures, and the dashboards/alerts recommended for early detection. ETBS is a lightweight lab and production-adjacent simulator used to reproduce tenant interactions that cause unexpected resource contention, KV-prefix cache thrashing, and indirect latency spikes. The goal is to reduce time-to-mitigate for hard-to-reproduce degradation patterns and to provide prescriptive operator actions tied to observable signals.

Scope:
- Systems covered: LLM inference runtime, KV prefix cache, batching layer, tenant priority queue, regional routing, and serving-side prefetch components.
- Environments: non-prod simulation cluster (primary), canary region, and production operator-guided sandbox.
- Not covered: client-side application bug...

title: Instrumentation Sunset and Cleanup Playbook
preview:
Overview
--------
This playbook describes the approved, low-risk process to retire (sunset) unused or harmful instrumentation signals, remove stale dashboards/alerts, and reclaim telemetry budget. It's intended for platform engineers, telemetry owners, and oncall operators. Follow the steps below to minimize alert c...
text:
Overview
--------
This playbook describes the approved, low-risk process to retire (sunset) unused or harmful instrumentation signals, remove stale dashboards/alerts, and reclaim telemetry budget. It's intended for platform engineers, telemetry owners, and oncall operators. Follow the steps below to minimize alert churn, SLO regressions, and data-loss surprises.

When to use this playbook
-------------------------
- A metric or log signal has no active consumer for >= 90 days.
- An alert fires repeatedly but has no owner or actionability (signal noise).
- A dashboard is irrelevant after a major architecture change (service split, deprecation).
- Telemetry cost budget is exhausted by high-cardinality data that provides low value.

Goals and constraints
---------------------
- Ensure no customer-facing SLOs are degraded by telemetry removal.
- Maintain auditability: every sunset must ha...

title: Optimization Config Service (OCS)
preview:
## Overview
Optimization Config Service (OCS) is the control-plane service responsible for **versioning, validating, and safely rolling out** optimization configuration changes across Redwood’s inference fleet.

Optimization configs cover settings that affect runtime economics and performance, including:
- **Batchin...
text:
## Overview
Optimization Config Service (OCS) is the control-plane service responsible for **versioning, validating, and safely rolling out** optimization configuration changes across Redwood’s inference fleet.

Optimization configs cover settings that affect runtime economics and performance, including:
- **Batching** (heuristics, max batch tokens/size, queue timeouts)
- **Caching** (prefix/KV cache enablement, cache sizing policies)
- **Quantization profiles** (profile selection, compatibility constraints, quality gating)

OCS implements:
- Immutable **Optimization Config Version** objects with metadata and diffs
- Safe apply workflows (validate → canary → promote)
- One-click rollback (Console + API)
- Audit logging and RBAC enforcement

OCS supports **Hosted** and **Dedicated** fully managed via Redwood control plane, and provides the same versioning model for **Private** deployme...

Summary:
This page provides a compact, operator-focused playbook for defining, instantiating, and using incident signal dashboards and alert templates. It includes recommended signal types, suggested threshold bands, dashboard panels to include in every incident view, a step-by-step triage workflow, and a small library of ready-to-copy panel snippets. Target audience: platform operators, oncall, SREs, and platform product managers who maintain the dashboard templates.

Why this exists:
- Reduce time-to-detection and time-to-resolution by standardizing which signals are visible to operators during an incident.
- Ensure dashboards prioritize operator decisions (triage, mitigate, route) rather than raw telemetry.
- Provide reproducible templates so teams can spin up region- or service-specific incident views quickly.

Scope and ownership:
- Applies to platform-level dashboards that infor...

title: Platform sidecar contracts and healthcheck standards
preview:
Summary

Purpose: Define a concise, enforceable contract for platform sidecars so that every service running on Redwood’s platform has consistent health checks, metrics, lifecycle behavior, and alerting. This reduces incidents caused by inconsistent sidecar implementations and speeds onboarding of new sidecars.

Sco...
text:
Summary

Purpose: Define a concise, enforceable contract for platform sidecars so that every service running on Redwood’s platform has consistent health checks, metrics, lifecycle behavior, and alerting. This reduces incidents caused by inconsistent sidecar implementations and speeds onboarding of new sidecars.

Scope: Applies to all new platform-managed sidecars (network proxies, auth-injectors, telemetry-forwarders, cache-warmers) deployed to Redwood clusters (hosted and Dedicated). Does NOT replace application-level probes; it standardizes sidecar behavior only.

Key goals
- Deterministic health endpoints and semantics (liveness, readiness, readiness gating).
- Minimum metric set and Prometheus labels for correlation.
- Standardized Kubernetes probe specs and resource budgets.
- Dashboard and alert templates for immediate on-call visibility.
- Clear ownership and onboarding/runbook...

title: Token Anomaly Forecasting Playbook
preview:
Summary
-----
This playbook documents a repeatable operator workflow for detecting, forecasting, and responding to token-anomaly events (sudden token bursts, slow token leaks, unexpected prefix growth) that materially affect runtime cost and SLOs. It includes a recommended dashboard layout, alerting thresholds, inve...
text:
Summary
-----
This playbook documents a repeatable operator workflow for detecting, forecasting, and responding to token-anomaly events (sudden token bursts, slow token leaks, unexpected prefix growth) that materially affect runtime cost and SLOs. It includes a recommended dashboard layout, alerting thresholds, investigation steps, automated mitigations, and a small roadmap for improving signal fidelity. The goal is to reduce mean time to detect (MTTD) for cost-impacting token anomalies and to provide safe mitigations that preserve customer-facing latency and correctness.

Scope and non-goals
------------------
- Scope: hosted + dedicated inference request paths, prefix/KV cache metrics, batching telemetry, token accounting (ingestion/egress), and associated billing signals.
- Non-goals: this playbook does not replace model-quality regression triage (use the ML evals runbook) and does...

title: Operator safe-mode and manual intervention playbook
preview:
Summary:
This playbook describes the criteria and step-by-step procedures for placing platform subsystems into Operator Safe-Mode (OSM), and for executing manual interventions when automated fallbacks are not sufficient. It is intended for platform operators, on-call engineers, and incident commanders. OSM is a grad...
text:
Summary:
This playbook describes the criteria and step-by-step procedures for placing platform subsystems into Operator Safe-Mode (OSM), and for executing manual interventions when automated fallbacks are not sufficient. It is intended for platform operators, on-call engineers, and incident commanders. OSM is a graded, reversible set of operational constraints designed to preserve critical SLOs while minimizing customer impact during complex multi-tenant degradation events.

Scope and goals:
- Scope: Redwood Inference runtime, routing layer, tenant-slicing, model-variant fallback, KV/prefix cache, and autoscaling control plane.
- Primary goal: protect foreground tail-latency SLOs for priority tenants and keep control plane safe to avoid cascading failures.
- Secondary goal: preserve cost controls and avoid unnecessary global rollbacks.

When to consider Operator Safe-Mode (triggers):
...

preview:

This document defines a minimal, testable telemetry contract and a step-by-step validation runbook for canarying LLM model variants within Redwood’s serving platform. It provides precise metrics to collect, dashboard and alert templates, acceptance thresholds for automated promotion, and operator pr...
text:
Summary
--------
This document defines a minimal, testable telemetry contract and a step-by-step validation runbook for canarying LLM model variants within Redwood’s serving platform. It provides precise metrics to collect, dashboard and alert templates, acceptance thresholds for automated promotion, and operator procedures for manual validation and rollback. The goal is to standardize canary rollouts so product and platform teams can safely verify model quality, latency and cost signals before broad promotion.

Scope
-----
- Applies to model variant rollouts across Hosted, Dedicated and Private deployments.
- Focuses on platform-observable signals (latency, error surface, token cost) and model-output quality signals surfaced via evaluation webhooks / retriever metrics.
- Does NOT replace domain-specific human-eval gating for regulated customers.

Key principles
--------------
- Obser...

text:
Summary:

This document defines the authoritative interservice dependency directory for platform services, the classification system we use to prioritize mitigation actions, and an operational playbook to prepare for and respond to cascading failures. It is intended for platform engineers, oncall, SREs, and service owners.

Goals:
- Provide a single source of truth for upstream/downstream relationships and ownership.
- Standardize detection, alerting, and automated containment actions for dependency-driven incidents.
- Document proactive chaos/DR experiments and continuous validation steps to reduce blast radius.
- Clarify runbook steps for fault isolation, traffic shepherding, and recovery.

Scope:
- Applies to all Redwood platform services in the hosted/dedicated/private runtime stacks (control plane services, routing, caching, model-proxy, prefix-cache, billing, telemetry ingestion...

title: Safe Apply + Rollback (Optimization Configs) — Internal Beta Retrospective
preview:

**Primary goals of the beta**
- Validate that config changes are **traceable, reversible, and safe to apply** under real operat...
text:
## Overview
This page captures what we learned from the **internal beta** of **Optimization Config versioning + Safe Apply + one-click Rollback**.

**Beta window:** 2026-01-06 → 2026-01-24

**Primary goals of the beta**
- Validate that config changes are **traceable, reversible, and safe to apply** under real operational conditions.
- Confirm **rollback reliability** under partial rollout, retries, and concurrent activity.
- Stress test **gating signals** (5xx + p95/p99 latency; eval/quality gates for quant profiles where applicable).
- Collect **Console usability feedback** from on-call (SRE + Platform + Runtime).

**Beta scope**
- Hosted and Dedicated internal tenants only (3 internal workloads; 6 models across 2 regions).
- Features enabled behind feature flags:
 - `opt_config_versioning`
 - `opt_safe_apply_controller`
 - `opt_console_rollback`
 - `opt_audit_events`

**Participants...

- Surface business impact (cost, affected customers, token loss) alongside technical signals.
- Provide deterministic operator steps for each priority to reduce cognitive load.

title: Operator Sandbox: Synthetic Scenarios & Verification Catalog
preview:
Overview
--------
This page is a curated catalog of synthetic, reproducible platform scenarios for the operator sandbox environment (sandbox.redwood.internal). The goal is to provide a single, searchable library of tests that operators and SREs can run to validate runtime behavior, fallback logic, and observability ...
text:
Overview
--------
This page is a curated catalog of synthetic, reproducible platform scenarios for the operator sandbox environment (sandbox.redwood.internal). The goal is to provide a single, searchable library of tests that operators and SREs can run to validate runtime behavior, fallback logic, and observability plumbing before changes reach staging or production.

Short intro: each entry is a self-contained scenario with intent, preconditions, steps to execute, expected signals, verification checks, and teardown instructions. Scenarios are designed to be idempotent and safe to run in the operator sandbox; some are safe to run in regional staging with approval.

Goals
-----
- Provide reproducible test cases for common and high-risk operational behaviors (eviction, batching regression, fallback misfire, cold-cache storms).
- Surface gaps in dashboards, alerting, and tracing by drivi...

title: Rolling Linux Kernel Patch Safety for GPU Fleet Ops
preview:
Overview:

Purpose: This runbook describes safe operational procedures, observability checks, and automated safeguards for performing rolling Linux kernel patches on Redwood GPU node pools. It focuses on minimizing P99/P99.9 latency blasts for inference traffic, preserving KV cache health, and maintaining SLOs durin...
text:
Overview:

Purpose: This runbook describes safe operational procedures, observability checks, and automated safeguards for performing rolling Linux kernel patches on Redwood GPU node pools. It focuses on minimizing P99/P99.9 latency blasts for inference traffic, preserving KV cache health, and maintaining SLOs during a multi-region rolling kernel update.

Scope: Applies to all GPU fleets that serve production inference (hosted + dedicated nodes) and any Kubernetes or VM groups running the Redwood serving runtime. Excluded: isolated on-prem clusters with dedicated change processes (see eng-private-deployments).

When to use this playbook:
- Security or stability kernel patches that must be applied within a maintenance window.
- Kernel backports that address scheduler, memory reclamation, or driver compatibility impacting inference performance.
- When automated node replacement is used ...

# Summary
We reduced the main integration risk by landing a low-overhead decision-event emission path and validating its performance impact.

- Control plane: implemented a basic policy evaluation endpoint returning canonical fallback_reason values.

# Risks / blockers
- Console policy editor still missing validation rules for incompatible model variants.

title: Hot-route capacity protection — Technical Design (Hosted API + Dedicated)
preview:
## 1. Summary
This document describes the end-to-end design for **hot-route capacity protection** across Redwood Inference’s **Hosted API** and **Dedicated** tiers.

The objective is to prevent a small set of high-volume, high-SLA routes (e.g., `/v1/chat/completions`, `/v1/embeddings`, `/v1/rerank`) from causing **f...
text:
## 1. Summary
This document describes the end-to-end design for **hot-route capacity protection** across Redwood Inference’s **Hosted API** and **Dedicated** tiers.

The objective is to prevent a small set of high-volume, high-SLA routes (e.g., `/v1/chat/completions`, `/v1/embeddings`, `/v1/rerank`) from causing **fleet-wide tail latency and 5xx cascades**. The system introduces:

- **Tier-aware rate limits and quotas** at the API gateway with consistent 429 semantics.
- **Priority classes** evaluated in a deterministic policy engine.
- **Admission control / controlled shedding** in routing based on real-time capacity signals.
- **Per-route budgets** that reserve capacity for enterprise traffic under pressure.
- **Observability and operational readiness** (metrics, dashboards, alerts, runbooks, incident-bot notifications).

This design is intentionally incremental: it does **not** rep...

title: Configuring priority routing and quotas (Hot-route capacity protection)
preview:
## Overview
This guide explains how to create and update **priority routing rules**, **quota/rate-limit definitions**, and **per-route overrides** for Hot-route capacity protection.

The primary goals of these configurations are:
- Preserve **Enterprise** latency/availability SLOs on protected routes during fleet/re...
text:
## Overview
This guide explains how to create and update **priority routing rules**, **quota/rate-limit definitions**, and **per-route overrides** for Hot-route capacity protection.

The primary goals of these configurations are:
- Preserve **Enterprise** latency/availability SLOs on protected routes during fleet/regional pressure.
- Ensure **Best-effort / Self-serve** traffic is throttled earlier and predictably.
- Keep overload behavior **deterministic, auditable, and supportable** (clear reason codes, logs, and dashboards).

This doc is written for operators in Platform, SRE, and on-call engineers who manage configs during rollouts, incident mitigation, and customer exception handling.

---

## Terminology
**Route**: An API endpoint (e.g., `POST /v1/chat/completions`, `POST /v1/embeddings`, `POST /v1/rerank`).

**Route group**: A logical grouping of routes that share protection beh...

preview:

Primary risk remains the runtime routing integration. We also uncovered a requirement gap around auditability of fallback decisions for enterprise customers.

- Completed design review: agreed to keep router decisioning local for latency, but emi...
text:
# Summary
Primary risk remains the runtime routing integration. We also uncovered a requirement gap around auditability of fallback decisions for enterprise customers.

# Status
- Overall: At Risk
- RAG: Red

# Progress this week
- Completed design review: agreed to keep router decisioning local for latency, but emit standardized decision events.
- Drafted event schema for policy evaluation and fallback decision logs.

# Risks / blockers
- Runtime team concern: additional per-request overhead for emitting decision events.
- Enterprise requirement: Dedicated customers want an auditable trail of why fallback occurred (capacity vs policy vs compatibility).

# Mitigations
- Emit decision events asynchronously and sample at 1% by default; allow customer-tier overrides.
- Add a "fallback_reason" enum and store only minimal fields required for audit logging.

# Next week
- Implement event em...

text:
## Purpose
This page specifies the dashboards and alerts used by on-call engineers to validate customer-facing impact at the **public status page component** level (product surface x region x capability). It is designed to:
- Reduce time-to-first-update (TTFU) by making component impact verification fast and repeatable.
- Improve status accuracy (avoid overly broad “API” incidents; avoid missing a single capability like Embeddings).
- Keep on-call burden low via standardized naming, deep links, and consistent thresholds.

This spec is the canonical reference for:
- Dashboard structure and naming conventions
- Required panels + tags/labels
- Alert routing and anti-flap behavior
- Runbook links and verification workflow

## Audience
- SRE on-call / Incident Commander (IC)
- Comms Lead (validates impacted components/regions before posting)

Overview:
This page defines a cross-stack failure-mode taxonomy, prescriptive architectural patterns to contain or degrade safely, and a concrete operator training pathway. It is intended for platform engineers, oncall operators, SREs, and platform product owners responsible for inference availability and cost-sensitive SLOs.

Purpose:
- Establish a common language for multi-layer failures (network, infra, runtime, model, data) so responders can triage quickly.
- Provide tested architectural mitigations and dashboard/alert specs that reduce mean time to repair (MTTR) and customer impact.
- Outline a progressive training program and gamedays to raise operator readiness.

Scope:
- Applies to Redwood-hosted runtime stacks (serving runtime, model runtime-proxy, KV cache, batcher, traffic router).
- Also covers interactions with Dedicated and Private control planes (where behavior differs)...

title: Operator-focused telemetry partitioning and lightweight agent playbook
preview:
Overview
--------
This playbook describes a low-risk, operator-focused approach to partitioning runtime telemetry for cost attribution and operator workflows. It prescribes a lightweight in-namespace agent pattern, concrete dashboard and alert definitions, and an operational rollout plan. The intent is to make cost ...
text:
Overview
--------
This playbook describes a low-risk, operator-focused approach to partitioning runtime telemetry for cost attribution and operator workflows. It prescribes a lightweight in-namespace agent pattern, concrete dashboard and alert definitions, and an operational rollout plan. The intent is to make cost signals actionable for operators while keeping cardinality and storage growth under control.

Goals
-----
- Provide operators with per-tenant and per-model cost telemetry that explains high-cost events within 5 minutes of occurrence.
- Keep high-cardinality labels out of hot Prometheus collections; use a partitioned timeseries + event-log pattern.
- Ensure safe rollout with feature flag, manual operator guardrails, and gradual backfill.

Scope
-----
- Applies to ingested runtime metrics (token counts, model selection, KV cache hits, batch sizes) and derived cost estimates.
...

text:
# Summary
Orion is progressing, but we have an emerging integration risk with the runtime router path for fallback decisions.

# Status
- Overall: At Risk
- RAG: Red

# Progress this week
- Policy engine skeleton merged behind a feature flag (orion_policy_engine_enabled).
- Defined fallback precedence rules for model variants (pinning > compatibility > cost).

# Risks / blockers
- Runtime router currently applies fallback in a different code path than the control plane expects; we may need a refactor or a shim layer.
- Timeline risk: if refactor is required, console beta slips by 1–2 weeks.

# Mitigations
- Schedule a 90-minute design review with runtime + platform owners.
- Implement a temporary adapter that maps router decisions into Orion policy evaluation events.

# Next week
- Prototype adapter in staging and validate event schema.

# Owners
- PM: Ava Chen
- Eng: Alex Martinez (p...

text:
# Summary
Project Orion is the cross-team effort to unify rollout controls (canary, A/B, and automatic fallback) into a single policy surface in the Redwood Console and routing layer.

# Status
- Overall: On Track
- RAG: Green

# Progress this week
- Landed initial API contract for policy evaluation (route selector -> policy engine).
- Console wireframes approved for the policy editor v1.
- Defined success criteria for v0 (single-model canary + fallback).

# Risks / blockers
- None material.

# Decisions
- Orion v0 will support canary by percentage and automatic rollback on 5xx rate regression.

# Next week
- Implement policy engine skeleton in platform-control-plane.
- Start integration with runtime router (read-only evaluation).

# Owners
- PM: Ava Chen
- Eng: Alex Martinez (platform), Maya Srinivasan (console)

title: Scheduler Class Metrics Dashboard Spec (Mixed-workload Scheduler Prototype)
preview:
## Overview
This page defines the Grafana dashboard specification for **per-class scheduler metrics** introduced by the mixed-workload scheduler prototype (short/long request classes). The dashboard is intended to make class-based queue health and fairness visible during:
- Development and load testing

text:
## Overview
This page defines the Grafana dashboard specification for **per-class scheduler metrics** introduced by the mixed-workload scheduler prototype (short/long request classes). The dashboard is intended to make class-based queue health and fairness visible during:
- Development and load testing
- Perf-canary and runtime release validation
- Production canaries (Hosted + Dedicated pools)
- Incident triage for tail-latency spikes under mixed prompt-length distributions

The dashboard focuses on:
- **Queue depth by class** (pressure)
- **Time in queue by class** (latency amplification)
- **Prefill vs decode breakdown** (where contention occurs)
- **Starvation/aging counters** (fairness regressions)
- **Dispatch share and token budget utilization** (policy effectiveness)

## Goals / non-goals
**Goals**
1. Provide a single “at-a-glance” view to answer: *Are short requests being pro...

title: Overload policy engine integration (Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding)
preview:
## Purpose
This document describes how overload protection policies are represented, validated, stored, and evaluated in the request path for the Hosted API. It is the integration reference for teams wiring circuit breakers + load shedding into:
- API Gateway admission middleware
- Smart Routing fallback decisions
-...
text:
## Purpose
This document describes how overload protection policies are represented, validated, stored, and evaluated in the request path for the Hosted API. It is the integration reference for teams wiring circuit breakers + load shedding into:
- API Gateway admission middleware
- Smart Routing fallback decisions
- Serving runtime backpressure/admission

Policies must be:
- **Tenant-aware** (per tenant overrides)
- **Tier-aware** (self-serve Hosted vs enterprise Hosted vs Dedicated)
- **Route/model-aware** (e.g., chat.completions vs embeddings; model family selectors)
- **Safe-by-default** (guardrails prevent unsafe configs)

This doc is implementation-oriented and complements ADR-015 (layering) and ADR-014 (customer error semantics).

---

## System overview
### Components
- **policy-engine** (Eng Platform): Policy evaluation service/library. Owns schema, validation, and evaluation ...

text:
## Purpose
This page specifies the dashboards and alert thresholds used to operate **Hosted overload protection** (circuit breakers + load shedding) across the request path (gateway → routing → runtime).

Goals:
- Detect overload early (before tail latency and timeouts spike).
- Make breaker/shed behavior observable, explainable, and safe to tune.
- Provide a single “what’s happening?” view during incidents (with links to runbooks and toggles).
- Keep **metric cardinality bounded** and alerts actionable (avoid paging for expected, controlled shedding).

Non-goals:
- Detailed policy design (see policy engine integration doc).
- Full per-tenant dashboards (only surfaced for targeted investigations; default views are tier/region/model).

## Audience
- SRE oncall, Platform oncall (gateway/policy), Smart Routing oncall, Serving Runtime oncall
- Support/CS (read-only): interpreting new over...

title: Operator Safety Net: Simulation & Training Playbook
preview:
Summary:

This playbook establishes a reproducible simulation framework and an operator training curriculum for proactive detection, mitigation, and escalation during multi-signal platform degradations (cache churn, batch spikes, GPU contention, and model fallback churn). The goal is to give operators a deterministi...
text:
Summary:

This playbook establishes a reproducible simulation framework and an operator training curriculum for proactive detection, mitigation, and escalation during multi-signal platform degradations (cache churn, batch spikes, GPU contention, and model fallback churn). The goal is to give operators a deterministic, hands-on safety net for exercising runbooks before incidents occur and to validate dashboard alerting/automations end-to-end.

Why this exists:

- Many incidents are caused by subtle multi-signal interactions that are hard to reproduce in production.
- Operators need repeatable, time-boxed exercises so oncall fatigue and cognitive load can be reduced.
- Verifying dashboards, runbooks, and automation together prevents false confidence in isolated tests.

Scope and non-goals:

- In scope: platform-level degradations that affect inference latency and token-cost SLOs for mul...

title: Model Registry Changelog Service (MRC): System Overview
preview:

This service is the canonical source for:

text:
## Summary
The **Model Registry Changelog Service** (service name: **mrc-changelog**) generates and serves **customer-visible** and **internal-only** model release notes from **model metadata change events** and **model version promotion events**.

This service is the canonical source for:
- A **chronological change history** for each served model and variant.
- **Release note artifacts** consumed by:
 - Redwood Console (Release Notes feed, per-model history)
 - Redwood Docs build (public release notes pages + feeds)
 - Support/internal tools (audit-driven investigations)

The service is explicitly designed to:
- Enforce **public vs internal field redaction**.
- Produce consistent categorization and language per the **Customer Release Notes taxonomy/style guide**.
- Provide **idempotent** generation (same events → same outputs) with traceability to schema hashes and eval evidence.

--...

title: Fleet Readiness Service (FRS) — Control Plane Service Documentation
preview:
## Overview
Fleet Readiness Service (FRS) is the Redwood control-plane component responsible for:
- Recording and serving the canonical **node readiness state** during fleet bring-up (Dedicated + shared capacity).
- Persisting **validation results and artifacts** produced by the provisioning orchestrator and node va...
text:
## Overview
Fleet Readiness Service (FRS) is the Redwood control-plane component responsible for:
- Recording and serving the canonical **node readiness state** during fleet bring-up (Dedicated + shared capacity).
- Persisting **validation results and artifacts** produced by the provisioning orchestrator and node validation agent.
- Exposing APIs to internal tools (Fleet UI, capacity ops tooling, SRE dashboards) to answer: “Is this node safe to join serving pools?” and “Why did it fail?”
- Emitting **telemetry** (metrics, logs, and traces) used for time-to-ready SLIs/SLOs and triage.

FRS is part of the Provision-to-Ready automation sprint and implements the readiness model defined in ADR-017.

---

## Service ownership and support
**Owner team:** Platform Control Plane (eng-platform)

**Primary on-call:** Platform Control Plane
- Escalation path: Platform on-call → Infra/Fleet on-cal...

## Purpose
This page is the catalog of **Grafana dashboards** and **alert rules** used to operate the Provision-to-Ready automation for Dedicated capacity and shared GPU fleet bring-up.

Dashboards are designed to answer:
- How long does it take to go from **Allocated → Ready** (overall + by step)?
- Where are we failing (step + error class), and is it isolated to a **region** or **GPU type**?
- Are nodes being **Quarantined** at an elevated rate, and what’s driving it?
- Which validation checks are **flaky** (pass/fail oscillation) vs consistently failing?

title: Capacity Policy Service (CPS) — Control Plane service for scaling policy evaluation
preview:
## Overview
Capacity Policy Service (CPS) is an internal control plane service responsible for storing, validating, and *deterministically evaluating* per-deployment scaling policies for **Redwood Dedicated**.

CPS exists to ensure consistent, auditable scaling semantics across:
- Console + API configuration (writes...
text:
## Overview
Capacity Policy Service (CPS) is an internal control plane service responsible for storing, validating, and *deterministically evaluating* per-deployment scaling policies for **Redwood Dedicated**.

CPS exists to ensure consistent, auditable scaling semantics across:
- Console + API configuration (writes)
- Fleet autoscaler loops (reads/evaluations)
- Smart routing and downstream consumers that need a canonical “bounded” capacity state

**Primary responsibilities**
1. Persist per-deployment scaling policy configuration (min/max bounds, cooldowns, predictive mode settings).
2. Provide an evaluation API that resolves **effective policy** (customer config + platform defaults + feature flags) and returns a canonical, deterministic result.
3. Emit audit events for config changes and evaluations (who/when/what/why) to support enterprise change management.
4. Provide caching and ...

title: Autoscaling Bounds Observability Dashboard Spec (Dedicated)
preview:

The dashboards are designed to answer four recurring operational questions:
1) What did the autoscaler want to do vs what it act...
text:
## Overview
This page specifies the Grafana dashboards required to operate **Dedicated autoscaling with customer-configurable min/max bounds** and optional **predictive scaling signals**.

The dashboards are designed to answer four recurring operational questions:
1) What did the autoscaler want to do vs what it actually did after applying bounds?
2) Are we saturating at the max bound (or failing to meet the min bound) and what is the customer impact?
3) Is predictive mode helping (earlier scale-up) or hurting (oscillation, overshoot, wrong forecasts)?
4) Can on-call and CS quickly drill down per customer/deployment/region to explain behavior with auditability?

## Audience
- SRE on-call (triage, incident response)
- Infra/Fleet (autoscaler behavior, capacity shortages)
- Platform/Control plane (policy evaluation, validation)
- Customer Success / TAM (customer-facing explanations and ...

title: Burst Admission Controller (BAC) Service
preview:
## Summary
The Burst Admission Controller (BAC) is an internal, regional control-plane service that makes deterministic, low-latency admission decisions for Dedicated customers attempting to exceed their reserved baseline capacity (“bursting”) by borrowing from Redwood-managed burst pools.

BAC’s core responsibiliti...
text:
## Summary
The Burst Admission Controller (BAC) is an internal, regional control-plane service that makes deterministic, low-latency admission decisions for Dedicated customers attempting to exceed their reserved baseline capacity (“bursting”) by borrowing from Redwood-managed burst pools.

BAC’s core responsibilities are:
- Enforce guardrails (tenant caps, max burst ratios, cooldown windows, per-pool saturation limits, global fleet safety caps).
- Apply fairness across tenants sharing a burst pool (weighted token-bucket / WFQ hybrid; see ADR for selection).
- Produce explainable denial reasons for customers and Support.
- Emit metering signals for billing and operational telemetry for SRE.

Non-goals:
- Scheduling/queuing long-running work (BAC returns immediate decisions; callers may implement client-side or gateway queuing/backoff).
- Cross-region bursting (out of scope for initial...

title: Multi-signal Priority Lattice and Decision Runbook
preview:
Summary:

This runbook defines a multi-signal prioritization lattice used by platform operators when multiple alerts and business signals fire simultaneously. The lattice converts heterogeneous signals (latency, token-errors, quality regressions, customer impact, payment deltas, and telemetry noise) into a ranked ac...
text:
Summary:

This runbook defines a multi-signal prioritization lattice used by platform operators when multiple alerts and business signals fire simultaneously. The lattice converts heterogeneous signals (latency, token-errors, quality regressions, customer impact, payment deltas, and telemetry noise) into a ranked action queue with clear ownership and operator instructions. The goal is to: reduce time-to-mitigation for high-impact events, limit unnecessary escalations, and preserve customer SLAs.

When to use:
- Multi-region or multi-model incidents where more than two distinct alert streams are active concurrently.
- When the root cause is unclear and immediate mitigation decisions are required.
- During Brownouts (degraded SLOs in single region) or Multi-tenant contention events.

Key definitions:
- Priority score: a weighted integer (0-100) combining signals in the table below.
- La...

title: Operator signal fatigue and automation adoption retrospective
preview:
Summary
-------
This retrospective reviews a 5-month program (Oct 2024 — Feb 2025) to reduce operator signal fatigue across the platform operator surface. The initiative combined (1) signal compaction rules, (2) lightweight automation runbooks, and (3) a phased alert rationalization. Goals: reduce actionable alert n...
text:
Summary
-------
This retrospective reviews a 5-month program (Oct 2024 — Feb 2025) to reduce operator signal fatigue across the platform operator surface. The initiative combined (1) signal compaction rules, (2) lightweight automation runbooks, and (3) a phased alert rationalization. Goals: reduce actionable alert noise by 60%, shorten average triage time by 35%, and increase operator satisfaction (measured via pulse survey) by 20%.

Context and motivation
----------------------
As our multitenant LLM inference platform scaled in 2024, the operator team reported increasing cognitive load: frequent low-value alerts, repeated manual mitigations for common prefixes/KV-cache issues, and friction in following complex manual runbooks. This created slow incident response and inconsistent mitigations. The program was funded as a cross-functional engineering + platform initiative to: 
- Priori...

The goal is to make burst behavior *observable, explainable, and safe*—with a fast path to detecting (1) pool saturation, (2) unfair/noisy-neighbor impact, (3) admis...
text:
h1. Overview
This page catalogs the Grafana dashboards and alert rules used to operate *Dedicated Burst Capacity* (shared Redwood-managed burst pools). The goal is to make burst behavior *observable, explainable, and safe*—with a fast path to detecting (1) pool saturation, (2) unfair/noisy-neighbor impact, (3) admission-control failures, and (4) metering/export issues.

*Primary audience:* SRE (on-call), Platform (admission controller + policy engine), Fleet (capacity owners), Smart Routing, and Support/CS (read-only views).

h2. Where to find dashboards
* Grafana folder: *Dedicated → Burst Capacity*
* Primary datasource: Prometheus (real-time) + ClickHouse (aggregates for reporting)
* Regions/hardware are first-class filters on every dashboard:
 * region (e.g., us-east, eu-west)
 * hardware_class (e.g., H100, L40S)
 * pool (string ID; see “pool_id” conventions below)

title: Quantization Profile Config Service (QPCS) — route-level settings, resolution, caching, and audit logging
preview:

The **Quantization Profile Config Service (QPCS)** is the control-plane service responsible for storing and resolving **route-level quantization settings** for Redwood Inference.

QPCS provides:
- A persisted **data model** for per-route quantization enablement.
- CRUD **internal APIs** used by the Conso...
text:
## Overview
The **Quantization Profile Config Service (QPCS)** is the control-plane service responsible for storing and resolving **route-level quantization settings** for Redwood Inference.

QPCS provides:
- A persisted **data model** for per-route quantization enablement.
- CRUD **internal APIs** used by the Console backend and automation.
- A deterministic **resolution path** used by the Routing/Policy Engine at request time.
- Multi-layer **caching** to protect the Model Registry and keep routing decisions low-latency.
- Structured **audit logging** for compliance and change control.

This doc describes QPCS APIs, data model, policy evaluation flow, caching, and audit logging.

---

## Goals and non-goals
### Goals
1. Support opt-in **route-level** enablement of a quantization profile for a model variant.
2. Ensure the resolved decision is **deterministic** for the same inputs (te...

title: Audit Exporter Service Architecture (SIEM-ready Audit Export Beta)
preview:
# Overview
The **Audit Exporter** is a multi-tenant service responsible for reliably delivering **Redwood audit events** to customer-controlled SIEM/logging destinations. This document describes the architecture for the **SIEM-ready audit export beta** (Hosted + Dedicated). Private/on-prem is explicitly out of scope...
text:
# Overview
The **Audit Exporter** is a multi-tenant service responsible for reliably delivering **Redwood audit events** to customer-controlled SIEM/logging destinations. This document describes the architecture for the **SIEM-ready audit export beta** (Hosted + Dedicated). Private/on-prem is explicitly out of scope for beta, but multi-region and deployment constraints are noted for follow-on.

**Primary goals**
- **At-least-once delivery** of audit events to configured destinations, with operator tooling for dead-letter/replay.
- **Configurable filtering** with safe defaults and clear semantics.
- **Operational visibility**: export lag, delivery success/error rates, per-destination health.
- **Security posture**: tenant isolation, least-privilege credential handling, PII minimization, egress controls.

**Non-goals (beta)**
- Exactly-once delivery guarantees (customers must deduplicat...

title: Runbook: Debugging Route Experiment Assignment & Split Anomalies
preview:
h1. Purpose
This runbook covers how to debug route-level experiment split anomalies and attribution integrity issues for Redwood Inference’s Route Experiments (A/B/n) framework. It is intended for on-call engineers and support escalations when a customer or internal team reports that variant traffic is skewed, stick...
text:
h1. Purpose
This runbook covers how to debug route-level experiment split anomalies and attribution integrity issues for Redwood Inference’s Route Experiments (A/B/n) framework. It is intended for on-call engineers and support escalations when a customer or internal team reports that variant traffic is skewed, stickiness appears unstable, or metrics are missing/misattributed.

This runbook focuses on:
* Skewed allocation (e.g., 90/10 expected, 99/1 observed)
* Sticky-key drift (same user flips variants over time or across regions)
* Cache/config propagation issues (routers using stale or missing experiment config)
* Attribution drop (requests routed correctly but experiment_id/variant_id missing in metrics/traces)

h1. Quick decision tree (first 10 minutes)
1) Confirm scope: single route vs multiple; single region vs global; all customers vs one tenant.
2) Identify symptom category:
 ...

## Overview
This document specifies the **Onboarding KPIs dashboard** used to track the Guided Onboarding + “First Production Launch” checklist initiative for Hosted API.

The dashboard answers:
- Are we improving **time-to-first-success (TTFS)** and **time-to-first-production (TTFP)**?
- Where do users drop off in the checklist funnel, and why?
- Are we introducing regressions (e.g., auth/rate-limit/streaming confusion) during rollout?

This spec covers:
- Metric definitions (TTFS/TTFP + funnel/step completion)
- Cohorts and filtering
- Drop-off reason taxonomy
- Alerts (thresholds, data quality, and regression detection)

## Audience
- DevEx, Hosted API PM, Console PM/design: adoption + activation
- Telemetry/Platform: instrumentation integrity + regressions
- Support/CS: early detection of common onboarding failures and documentation gaps

- Host...

 - Which Dedicated deployments are currently below their committed throughput floor?

text:
## Overview
This page specifies the standard Grafana dashboards and alerting rules for Route Experiments (route-level A/B/n testing) in Redwood Inference. The goal is to provide a consistent operational view across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments, with minimal onboarding work for SRE/on-call and customer-facing Console support.

The dashboards/alerts defined here cover:
- Per-variant guardrails: latency, cost, error rate, throughput
- Control-plane config propagation lag/staleness
- Attribution drop rate (missing {experiment_id, variant_id})
- “Variant 0 traffic” detection (variant assigned allocation but receiving zero/near-zero requests)

## Audience
- SRE and on-call engineers responding to experiment-related incidents
- Platform/Policy Engine engineers validating routing correctness
- Telemetry & Analytics engineers monitoring attribution integrity
